page: Whether the Episcopal Consecrations Performed by the Society of Saint Pius X on July 1, 2026 Likewise Constituted a Schismatic Act.
heading: Reply to Objection 3. Ongoing Dispute Does Not Make the Charge False
text: See Objection Theological and canonical dispute about the consecrations does not dissolve the schismatic character of the act. The existence of SSPX canonical studies and sympathetic academic commentary does not change what Pope John Paul II declared, what Pope Benedict XVI maintained even while remitting the personal censures — that the Society has no canonical status and its ministers exercise no legitimate ministry — and what Leo XIV has now reiterated. The Bisig essay noted in 1988 that the SSPX's canonical defense failed to cite 'one single recognized theologian or Father of the Church, or a single pontifical or conciliar document' acknowledging the legitimacy of episcopal consecration against the Pope's express will — and thirty-eight years of effort have not produced such a citation. 24 Debate does not create equal probability on both sides when the weight of the tradition, the consistent papal position, and the divine-constitution argument from Pius XII and Vatican I all point in one direction. As the Bisig/de Blignières essay concludes: 'The Society has rejected the obvious solution because it appears in contradiction with the analysis done...This solution consists on [sic] confidence in the promises of Christ to his Church: visibility, petrinity, indefectibility, and formal apostolicity.' 25 The Society’s own July documents confirm this rather than unsettle it. Neither the homily nor the Superior General’s reply of 30 June engages the canonical charge on its merits: neither mentions Canon 1387 or Canon 1364, nor the warning that the act would be schismatic, but answers throughout at the level of spiritual self-justification — which declines the charge rather than rebutting it. 26 And the Society continues to read 1988 as vindication, not error. Fr. Pagliarani asks the Pope to find in the mere continuation of dialogue ‘proof that the Society is neither schismatic nor hostile to the Church,’ ( Appendix A, A.2 ) and in the homily he holds up Archbishop Lefebvre — ‘not yet canonised’ — as the model the new bishops must follow, adding that ‘thirty-eight years ago, they condemned a saint.’ ( Appendix A, A.3 ) A body that treats the condemned act of 1988 as the pattern for 2026 has not resolved the dispute in its favor; it has chosen to repeat it. That serious men dispute the charge does not make it false, and the Society’s own witness shows the dispute is not over what was done, but over whether the Apostolic See had authority to forbid it. Return to top

page: Whether the Episcopal Consecrations Performed by the Society of Saint Pius X on July 1, 2026 Likewise Constituted a Schismatic Act.
heading: Reply to Objection 4. Inconsistent Enforcement Does Not Void the Canon
text: See Objection The double-standard argument has rhetorical force and genuine pastoral sting, and intellectual honesty requires acknowledging that Rome's inconsistent enforcement of Canon 1387 (1382) creates real credibility problems. Nevertheless the argument fails on three grounds. First, the cases are not canonically parallel, and not for the reason sometimes claimed. Cardinal Slipyj was not physically prevented from reaching Rome — he had lived there since 1963, fourteen years before the 1977 consecrations. The real disanalogies are three. Rome never called it schism : no decree ever characterized Slipyj's act as schismatic, and no excommunication was ever declared or applied — a fact SSPX's own commentators have called puzzling, but which is undisputed. The manner was opposite : Slipyj acted secretly, precisely to avoid a refusal he anticipated; the SSPX acted at a five-hour public ceremony before some 15,000 people, in explicit, publicized defiance of Leo XIV's personal, written request — days before the fact — that they reconsider. The emergency was categorically different : the Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church was then illegal, underground, and facing the death of its persecuted hierarchy, with no signed accord, no structural offer, and no functioning channel of communication with Rome — every one of which the SSPX had, and set aside. A quiet act of succession-planning by an elderly confessor of an outlawed Church, never called schism and never punished, is not a precedent for a public, defiant act that Rome declared schismatic within twenty-four hours. 27 Second , the Chinese case is more complex than the objection presents, but it actually strengthens rather than weakens the contrary position. The 2018 Sino-Vatican agreement was itself an attempt to bring Chinese episcopal consecrations within a framework of at least partial papal involvement — an acknowledgment by Rome that the unauthorized consecrations were problematic and required regularization, not a declaration that they were canonically equivalent to authorized ones. When Leo XIV subsequently accepted bishops consecrated in violation of that agreement, he did so in the context of a live diplomatic process aimed at eventual full regularization — not as a declaration that Canon 1387 (1382) does not apply or that the consecrations were unobjectionable. More pointedly: the Chinese bishops operate under a Communist state that does not recognize the Pope's authority over internal Chinese affairs — they act within a system of coercion that, whatever its moral failures, is not a free choice to defy papal authority. The SSPX, operating freely in Switzerland and multiple democratic countries, with full access to Rome, with multiple offers of regularization on the table, faces no comparable external constraint. The comparison flatters the SSPX by conflating coerced irregularity with freely chosen defiance. 28 Third , and most importantly, the argument proves too much. If inconsistent enforcement of a canon by ecclesiastical authority dissolved the canon's binding force, then every canonical penalty would be voidable simply by identifying another case where the same penalty was not imposed — a principle that would dissolve canonical order entirely. The Church's failure to apply Canon 1387 (1382) consistently in every case does not change what the canon requires or what the act objectively is. It may give rise to legitimate criticism of Rome's diplomatic priorities, and that criticism is not without weight. But canonical inconsistency is a failure of enforcement, not a determination of canonical truth. The SSPX's act is what it is regardless of what Rome has done or not done in Beijing. The double standard, if real, is Rome's problem to answer for — it is not a license for the SSPX to proceed as though the canon does not exist. 29 Return to top

page: Whether the Episcopal Consecrations Performed by the Society of Saint Pius X on July 1, 2026 Likewise Constituted a Schismatic Act.
heading: Reply to Objection 5. Necessity Admits No Degrees; the Necessity Has Lessened Since 1988, Not Grown; and the Society Cannot Be Judge of Its Own Necessity
text: See Objection Although we might grant the premise for the sake of argument, the conclusion still does not follow. First, the objection mistakes the structure of the principle it invokes. Necessity, as used in the objection, becomes a sliding scale: a graver crisis licenses a graver remedy. However no such scale exists. Article 2 has established that the requirement of the pontifical mandate belongs to the divine constitution of the Church, which necessity cannot override (Article 2, Replies to Objections 2 and 4), and the Pontifical Council for the Interpretation of Legislative Texts articulated the consequence exactly: a state of necessity "must be verified objectively," and "there is never a necessity to ordain Bishops contrary to the will of the Roman Pontiff." 30 Never is not a proportion. An argument that the crisis has worsened adds weight to a scale the Church teaches does not exist; it cannot make licit in 2026 what was excluded in principle in 1988, any more than a greater anger licenses the murder of one’s enemies. Second, the objection conflates the general crisis of the Church with the particular necessity the consecrations purport to remedy — the continuation of traditional priestly formation and sacramental life — and on the particular point the facts run opposite to the objection's premise. In 1988 that provision scarcely existed outside Écône; in 2026 it exists worldwide, for the Holy See herself, in the wake of 1988, provided for it through the Priestly Fraternity of Saint Peter, the Institute of Christ the King, and the Institute of the Good Shepherd. As Fr. Gerald Murray observes, consecrating bishops against the will of the Pope is a necessary means of providing new priests "only if it plans to continue operating apart from the Holy See." 31 Nor is this a partisan observation: the Society's own defenders concede that this line of thinking is common precisely among traditionalists and SSPX sympathizers who reject the 2026 consecrations. 32 The Society itself does not deny the fact: at the same roundtable, its own moderator granted that "today, undoubtedly more than in 1988," numerous communities throughout the world benefit from the Traditional Mass, and the Superior General's answer was not that such provision does not exist but that it lacks "guarantees" — that these communities live under a "Sword of Damocles" of episcopal discretion. 33 But this quietly replaces the necessity pleaded with a different one: no longer the want of the sacraments and of traditional formation, which is conceded to be supplied, but the want of a guarantee of permanent independence from the judgment of the hierarchy. That is not a necessity the Church could ever supply without ceasing to govern; it is a demand for the very autonomy whose assertion constitutes the schismatic character of the act. Whatever may be said of doctrinal confusion in general, the specific want the consecrations claim to answer has lessened since 1988, not grown — and what remains of it is the Society's own choice. Third, the objection cannot answer the question its own advocates have posed: who declares the state of necessity? 34 If the gravity of the crisis is the measure, the measurement rests with the party seeking the exemption — and the worse the Society judges the crisis, the more completely it has substituted its own judgment for the Church's. Carried to its term, the argument proves that the Church has failed in her ordinary mission and must be rescued by an extraordinary one — the very conclusion Pius XII condemned when the identical plea of necessity was made for the Chinese consecrations, 35 and the one the founders of the Fraternity of Saint Peter refused in 1988 by placing their confidence instead in the promises of Christ to His Church, as Reply to Objection 3 has already recalled. A necessity whose existence, gravity, and remedy are all certified by the party it exempts is not necessity but sovereignty. Fourth, whatever ambiguity attended 1988, none attended 2026. The consecrations followed despite a personal plea of Leo XIV, who explicitly named "the schismatic act you are about to undertake" ( Appendix A, A.1 ). A worsened crisis might in some other matter bear on imputability; it cannot convert an act performed after explicit papal prohibition into anything but deliberate choice. Necessity describes the absence of alternatives; contumacy describes their refusal. Return to top ← Article 2 Contents Article 4 →

text: Article 4 Whether the latae sententiae Excommunications Declared by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith on 2 July 2026 Were Validly Incurred and Canonically Effective.

page: Whether the latae sententiae Excommunications Declared by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith on 2 July 2026 Were Validly Incurred and Canonically Effective.
heading: Objection 1. Illicit Episcopal Consecration Is an Offense Against Sacraments, Not Schism
text: The consecration of a bishop without a pontifical mandate is penalized under Canon 1387 (1382) 1 — a delict classified among offenses against the sacraments and the usurpation of ecclesiastical functions, not among offenses against the unity of the Church, where schism (Canon 1364, defined by Canon 751) is located. Until the 1983 Code such consecrations were punished by suspension alone, and, as Bishop Schneider observes, ‘suspension is not an expression of schism.’ 2 The two delicts are, in the Society’s own canonical study, ‘totally independent things which by their very nature are not related.’ 3 By declaring the consecration ‘an act of a schismatic nature’ and invoking Canon 1364 alongside Canon 1387, the decree of 2 July 2026 conflates two distinct delicts; the external act of consecration alone cannot constitute schism, and the schism-based excommunication is therefore invalidly attached. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the latae sententiae Excommunications Declared by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith on 2 July 2026 Were Validly Incurred and Canonically Effective.
heading: Objection 2. The Excommunications Are Invalid Because Grave Necessity Reduces or Excuses From the Penalties of Law
text: Furthermore, no one incurs a latae sententiae penalty who violates a law under grave necessity or with diminished imputability (Canon 1323), 4 and Canon 1324 provides that attenuating circumstances — including even culpable error concerning the law — reduce or replace the penalty, so that it is not incurred automatically. The Society asserts an objective state of grave necessity for the salvation of souls, documented across decades of refused regularization; and even were that necessity judged only subjectively perceived, the Code protects the good-faith actor. On this reasoning the Society’s canonical study concludes that a latae sententiae excommunication declared ‘in contempt of’ Canons 1323 and 1324 must be held ‘completely invalid,’ 5 with the intrinsic nullity of all its effects. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the latae sententiae Excommunications Declared by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith on 2 July 2026 Were Validly Incurred and Canonically Effective.
heading: Objection 3. The Decree Exceeds the Canons Invoked
text: Furthermore, some canonists have argued that the decree over-reaches the penal law it invokes. 6 Penal laws are subject to strict interpretation (Canon 18), and imputability must be verified individually (Canons 1321–1325); a general declaratory decree can establish neither the strict applicability of the penalty nor the individual imputability of the many priests and lay faithful it purports to reach. On this view the decree’s extension of excommunication ‘from now on’ to all the Society’s clergy and to formally adhering laity declares more than the canons, strictly construed, actually impose, so that the extension at least is canonically ineffective. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the latae sententiae Excommunications Declared by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith on 2 July 2026 Were Validly Incurred and Canonically Effective.
heading: Objection 4. The SSPX Does Not Have a Schismatic Intention
text: Furthermore, schism under Canon 751 requires not merely an external act but a pertinacious refusal of submission to the Roman Pontiff — an animus schismaticus . The Society names the reigning Pontiff in the Canon of the Mass, prays for him, professes his primacy in principle, and has repeatedly sought canonical regularization. The interior element schism requires is therefore wanting, and Bishop Schneider had already judged, months before the decree issued, that any such excommunication would be 'of doubtful validity.’ 7 Where the essential element of the delict is absent, the penalty attached to that delict cannot validly be incurred. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the latae sententiae Excommunications Declared by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith on 2 July 2026 Were Validly Incurred and Canonically Effective.
heading: Objection 5. The July 11, 2026 Recourse Suspends Any Canonical Penalties
text: Furthermore, the Society has not accepted the decree as final. On July 11, 2026 it lodged a preliminary recourse with the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith, invoking canon 1734. 8 The Society's own communiqué states that this recourse "has the effect of suspending the execution of the decree, in accordance with canon 1353." 9 If the decree's execution is suspended by the Society's recourse, then whatever the merits of the underlying delict, the decree's declared excommunications are not presently in force against the named bishops — and a fortiori against the rest of the clergy and faithful. The question of validity examined above is therefore premature. For now, the censures are canonically not in effect. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the latae sententiae Excommunications Declared by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith on 2 July 2026 Were Validly Incurred and Canonically Effective.
heading: On the contrary, (The 1996 PCILT Note, Readopted in 2026, Has Already Judged the Necessity, Validity, and Mitigating-Circumstance Questions)
text: The Pontifical Council for the Interpretation of Legislative Texts, in its Explanatory Note of 24 August 1996 10 — which the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith expressly adopts ‘as its own’ in the decree of 2 July 2026 — holds that the state of necessity ‘must be verified objectively,’ that ‘there is never a necessity to ordain Bishops contrary to the will of the Roman Pontiff,’ and that ‘doubt cannot reasonably be cast upon the validity of the excommunication,’ finding no ‘exempting or lessening circumstances’ available in law. The competent authority has already judged the very questions the objections raise. I answer that The excommunications declared on 2 July 2026 were validly incurred. A latae sententiae penalty is incurred ipso facto by the commission of the delict itself; the decree is declaratory, not constitutive — it recognizes and publishes for the external forum a penalty already incurred by the act of 1 July, not one the Dicastery has newly imposed. The objections misclassify the act, misapply the mitigating canons, or misconceive the declaratory character of the decree. Return to top

page: Whether the latae sententiae Excommunications Declared by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith on 2 July 2026 Were Validly Incurred and Canonically Effective.
heading: Reply to Objection 1. The Location of the Canons for Consecration of a Bishop and Schism Does Not Alone Determine the Nature of the Crime
text: See Objection The location of a delict in the Code’s table of contents does not determine its nature. As Fr. Gerald Murray observes, 11 Canon 751 defines schism as the refusal of submission to the Roman Pontiff, and a consecration performed against the Pontiff’s explicit will is such a refusal in the order of act, whatever heading the penal canon for unauthorized consecration happens to occupy. The decree does not conflate two delicts; it recognizes that one act violates two distinct canons — Canon 1387 (1382) as an unauthorized consecration, and Canon 1364 as a schismatic act, since the disobedience ‘implies in practice the rejection of the Roman primacy’ ( Ecclesia Dei adflicta 3). The objection’s own premise is mistaken: the penalty of excommunication for unmandated consecration is not a creature of the 1983 Code but of the Holy Office’s decree of 1951, issued by Pope Pius XII in the face of the Chinese consecrations — the same pontiff who taught that the mandate requirement belongs to the Church’s divine constitution ( Article 2, Reply to Objection 4 ). The grade of penalty has varied with the gravity of the threat; the character of the act has not. That character rests on the divine constitution of the episcopate, not on the penal classification. Return to top

page: Whether the latae sententiae Excommunications Declared by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith on 2 July 2026 Were Validly Incurred and Canonically Effective.
heading: Reply to Objection 2. Canons 1323–1324 Do Not Excuse an Act Against a Personal Papal Prohibition
text: See Objection The mitigating canons protect one who acts under a genuine and objective necessity, or under a non-culpable error; neither obtains here. The 1996 Note, now readopted, foreclosed the necessity plea for precisely this delict, since an act against ‘the very foundations of [ecclesial] unity’ cannot be one the law excuses, and no attenuating circumstance was found admissible. The necessity was in any case manufactured by the refusal of available regularization (Articles 1–3), and the act followed the explicit, personal, and repeated prohibition of the Roman Pontiff, which forecloses even the plea of putative necessity, for one cannot claim to have reasonably believed circumstances excused an act after the legislator himself, personally engaged, judged in the very moment that they did not; and it removes any vestige of the ignorance or error on which the remaining exemptions turn. As for the claim that a penalty ‘declared in contempt of’ those canons is intrinsically null: the judgment whether they apply belongs to the competent authority, which has made it; a private party cannot annul the supreme authority’s penal declaration by asserting its own contrary reading of the mitigating circumstances. Even were the sanction thought unjust, it would not thereby be void: as the canonist Marc Balestrieri observes, recalling the classical principle, an excommunication that is unjust but not null is still to be feared until it is lawfully annulled. 12 Return to top

page: Whether the latae sententiae Excommunications Declared by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith on 2 July 2026 Were Validly Incurred and Canonically Effective.
heading: Reply to Objection 3. A Declaratory Decree Needs No Individualized Process — the Law Is Clear, Not Doubtful
text: See Objection The objection misconceives the decree’s juridical character. A latae sententiae excommunication is incurred by the delict itself; the decree declares it, and a declaratory act neither imposes a penalty nor requires the individualized penal process proper to a ferendae sententiae sentence. Strict interpretation (Canon 18) governs the scope of a penal law, not whether a clearly applicable law applies: Canon 1364 §1 plainly attaches excommunication to schism, and Canon 1387 (1382) to consecration without mandate, and the act falls within the plain terms of both — there is no ambiguity to be construed narrowly. Individualized imputability (Canons 1321–1325) is presumed upon an external violation (Canon 1321 §4) and remains open to rebuttal in the individual case; the decree’s general statement that clergy and formally adhering laity ‘would incur’ the penalty states the law rather than adjudicating any individual’s imputability, which is determined in the proper forum under the two-element test of ‘formal adherence’ set out in the 1996 Note. This is consistent with the preceding Reply: what the competent authority has definitively judged is that no circumstance excuses the delict as such — that there is never a necessity to consecrate against the Roman Pontiff’s will. Whether a particular individual’s imputability was diminished remains, as always, examinable in the proper forum; what may not be done is to convert that individual question into a general suspension of a penalty the law itself attaches. 13 Return to top

page: Whether the latae sententiae Excommunications Declared by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith on 2 July 2026 Were Validly Incurred and Canonically Effective.
heading: Reply to Objection 4. Pertinacity Is Established Objectively, Not by Professed Good Will
text: See Objection The pertinacity Canon 751 requires is established by the doing of the act after explicit and repeated prohibition, not by the offender’s profession of good will. As Article 2 established, the animus Canon 751 requires is located in the determinate act itself; a consecration carried out against the Pontiff’s personal appeal is the refusal of submission, and no affirmation of the primacy ‘in principle’ undoes it. The Society’s own declaration read in place of the apostolic mandate — that ‘every punishment and censure brought to bear against this step will have no validity’ ( Appendix A, A.4 ) — is itself a public rejection of the Church’s disciplinary authority, and so evidence of the very animus the objection denies. Bishop Marian Eleganti, replying directly to the contrary view, holds that the absence of a declared intention to break with Rome does not alter the objective nature of the act, and that to plead a benign intention is a way of deceiving people about the objective schismatic nature and effect of the disobedience; 14 Cardinal Müller judges the matter likewise, by objective criteria, not subjective judgments. 15 Bishop Schneider’s contrary judgment — expressed before the decree issued, that an excommunication in these circumstances would be of ‘doubtful validity’ — rests on treating the Society’s professed intentions as decisive, and is weighed and respectfully set aside: it rests on treating the interior element as unestablished, whereas the external act, done in these circumstances, supplies precisely the evidence the law admits. Return to top

page: Whether the latae sententiae Excommunications Declared by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith on 2 July 2026 Were Validly Incurred and Canonically Effective.
heading: Reply to Objection 5. Suspending the Decree's Declaration Does Not Undo the ipso facto Penalty — and the Recourse Itself Concedes the Forum
text: See Objection The objection proves less than it claims, because it misjudges the scope of canon 1353. That canon suspends the execution of a decree pending recourse; it does not suspend a penalty the decree never constituted. As established above, a latae sententiae excommunication is incurred ipso facto by the commission of the delict itself — the consecrations of 1 July — and the decree of 2 July is declaratory, not constitutive: it recognizes and declares in the external forum a penalty that already exists. 16 Recourse under canon 1734 is addressed to the issuing authority and seeks the decree's revocation or amendment. 17 Nor does the canon’s word ‘declare’ carry the Society’s conclusion. Recourse against a declaratory decree suspends the decree’s execution — that is, the aggravated effects Canon 1331 §2 attaches to a declared excommunication as distinct from an undeclared one. What it cannot suspend is the penalty itself, which the decree did not create: a latae sententiae excommunication exists by operation of law from the moment of the delict, and no procedural act directed at the declaration can reach back to it. At its theoretical maximum, then, the Society’s recourse restores its bishops to their position of July 1 — excommunicated by the law itself, but not yet declared so. The communiqué claims a relief the canon cannot give. 18 In fact, the recourse undermines the Society's position rather than strengthening it. One does not seek revocation of an act that, according to them, has no legal consequence. In lodging recourse under canons 1734 and 1353, the Society treats the decree as juridically operative and worth contesting through the Church's own administrative process. This is itself an implicit acknowledgment of the ecclesiastical forum's competence over the question, the same competence this Article has argued the Society cannot simultaneously invoke and reject. Whatever the outcome of the recourse — whether the decree is confirmed, corrected, or revoked by the competent authority — that outcome will be a judgment about the declaration; it cannot reach back and undo the ipso facto excommunication the declaration merely recorded. Return to top ← Article 3 Contents Article 5 →

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
text: Article 5 Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
heading: Objection 1. Because a Priest Says Mass in the Name of the Church, Faithful Do Not Sin by Receiving From a Sinful Priest.
text: A sinful priest in good canonical standing celebrates Mass on behalf of the Church. His personal sin does not communicate to the faithful because they receive Communion not from him personally but from the Church whose minister he is. The same sacramental act occurs at an SSPX Mass — valid matter, valid form, valid orders. The faithful therefore receive from Christ through a validly ordained priest and do not participate in any irregularity. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
heading: Objection 2. Hawaii Five and Msgr. Perl Decisions Say This Is Permitted
text: Furthermore, Cardinal Ratzinger ruled in the case of the Hawaii Five that attendance at an SSPX chapel does not constitute the canonical crime of schism. 1 And in 2003 Msgr. Camille Perl of the Pontifical Commission Ecclesia Dei wrote that the faithful can fulfill their Sunday obligation at an SSPX Mass and that a modest contribution to the collection is not sinful. 2 If the future Pope Benedict XVI ruled that attendance is canonically insufficient for schism, it cannot constitute mortal sin to receive Communion at such a Mass. Canon law and moral theology ought to be consistent. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
heading: Objection 3. Due to Necessity, Supplied Jurisdiction, and/or epikeia Attendance Is Licit
text: Furthermore, the Society’s canonical defense rests on theologians and canonists of high standing — Prümmer, Roberti, Palazzini — whose principles of supplied jurisdiction it applies to its own case, and contemporary commentators in full communion have endorsed the application: SSPX sacraments, on this reasoning, are licitly attended on the basis of supplied jurisdiction, epikeia , or necessity. Where an application of received principles is defended by serious authors, a probable opinion exists in moral theology. 3 Acting on a probable opinion is generally licit. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
heading: Objection 4. Pope Francis’ Faculties for Confessions and Marriage Prove Church Tolerates SSPX Sacraments
text: Furthermore, Pope Francis's permanent grant of supplied jurisdiction for absolution in SSPX confessionals suggests Rome does not regard SSPX sacramental ministry as something the faithful must categorically avoid. If Rome wanted the faithful to abstain entirely from SSPX sacraments, it would not have institutionalized access to them. 4 See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
heading: Objection 5. If the Church Permits Reception from Eastern Orthodox, She A Fortiori Permits Sacraments From the SSPX
text: The Church does not categorically forbid communicatio in sacris with those outside full communion. Canon 844 §2 5 explicitly permits the faithful to receive penance, the Eucharist, and anointing of the sick from non-Catholic ministers whose churches possess valid sacraments — precisely the situation of Eastern Orthodox clergy — whenever necessity or genuine spiritual advantage commends it, provided danger of error or indifferentism is avoided and approach to a Catholic minister is physically or morally impossible. This was applied concretely to the faithful behind the Iron Curtain and in regions such as Siberia, where Catholic clergy were inaccessible for extended periods. The principle establishes that access to valid sacraments can take precedence over strict ecclesial boundaries. If this is true of the Orthodox — a body in formal, centuries-old schism that does not even nominally recognize the Roman primacy — it applies with greater force to the SSPX. SSPX priests are not ministers of a separated church; they are validly ordained Catholic priests who profess the whole of Catholic doctrine, including the papal primacy in principle, differing from clergy in good standing only by a canonical irregularity that is itself disputed ( Article 1 ). If the faithful may licitly receive from an Orthodox priest who denies the dogma of the papacy outright, it is incongruous to forbid reception from an SSPX priest who affirms it. Further, ‘moral impossibility’ should be read pastorally, not narrowly. For many traditionally-minded Catholics, the only Mass reasonably accessible is the Novus Ordo , which they hold — on grounds shared by clergy in good standing — to be liturgically and doctrinally compromised. Moral impossibility has long included grave inconvenience, not only physical distance; being unable in good conscience to participate in a liturgy one holds gravely deficient is arguably such a hardship. On this reading, the faithful with only the Novus Ordo available are not unlike the faithful behind the Iron Curtain: a Catholic sacrament is nominally present, but burdensome enough to justify seeking it elsewhere. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
heading: Objection 6. Canon 1335 Lifts Censures When Faithful Legitimately Request the Sacraments
text: Furthermore, the Code itself resolves the question in favor of the faithful. Canon 1335 §2 (1335) provides that if a censure prohibits the celebration of sacraments or sacramentals, the prohibition is suspended whenever this is necessary to care for the faithful in danger of death; and that if a latae sententiae censure has not been declared, the prohibition is moreover suspended whenever a member of the faithful requests a sacrament, a sacramental, or an act of governance — which request the faithful may make for any just cause. 6 Whatever the force of the argument from suspension ( Article 1 ), no decree of suspension names the individual SSPX priest specifically; the priest ordained at Écône in 2015 or 2020 incurred his censure, if at all, latae sententiae and undeclared. The Decree of July 2, 2026 confirms this on its face: it declares censures for six bishops alone, and warns clerics and lay faithful only in the conditional — that they would incur excommunication by adhering to the schism — thereby acknowledging that they have not yet incurred it. 7 Penal laws are of strict interpretation (Canon 18), and the accompanying Nota Esplicativa's present-tense assertion that the Society's ministers are in schism cannot enlarge what the Decree, the only instrument in penal form, actually declares. Nor can the 1996 Note supply the defect: it is not an authentic interpretation under Canon 16 §1, never having received specific pontifical approval, but an opinion of the Pontifical Council published in Communicationes . 8 When the faithful approach him and request Communion, the law itself lifts his prohibition for the duration of that act. The Church, through this canon, tolerates the minister in precisely the moment of ministry at issue. Aquinas's principle therefore does not apply: the faithful who request a sacrament using this canon do not communicate in the sin of a minister whom the Church Herself, through this canon, explicitly permits to minister to the one requesting. The devout desire for the Traditional Mass and sacraments is manifestly a just cause, and nothing in the canon confines it to a single occasion. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
heading: On the contrary, (A Man Receiving From a Non-Tolerated Minister Communicates in His Sin)
text: St. Thomas Aquinas teaches in the Summa Theologiae III, Q.64, A.6, Reply to Objection 2: 9 'He who approaches a sacrament receives it from a minister of the Church, not because he is such and such a man, but because he is a minister of the Church. Consequently, as long as the latter is tolerated in the ministry, he that receives a sacrament from him does not communicate in his sin, but communicates with the Church from whom he has his ministry. But if the Church, by degrading, excommunicating, or suspending him, does not tolerate him in the ministry, he that receives a sacrament from him sins, because he communicates in his sin.' And in the same treatise Saint Thomas states the conclusion in its own terms, Summa Theologiae III, Q.82, A.9: 'Heretical, schismatical, excommunicate, or even sinful priests, although they have the power to consecrate the Eucharist, yet they do not make a proper use of it; on the contrary, they sin by using it. But whoever communicates with another who is in sin, becomes a sharer in his sin. Consequently, it is not lawful to receive Communion from them, or to assist at their Mass. Still there is a difference among the above, because heretics, schismatics, and excommunicates, have been forbidden, by the Church's sentence, to perform the Eucharistic rite. And therefore whoever hears their Mass or receives the sacraments from them, commits sin.' SSPX priests exercise ministry outside the Church's hierarchical communion, under the suspension established in Article 1: 10 the 1976 suspension a divinis was formally declared and personally communicated, in a matter Pope Paul VI had explicitly framed in terms of the dimissorial letters requirement; Benedict XVI confirmed in 2009 that SSPX ministers do not legitimately exercise ministry in the Church. The 1988 episcopal consecrations were declared a schismatic act by Pope John Paul II in Ecclesia Dei adflicta; Cardinal Gantin's decree of excommunication, issued the previous day, cited Canon 1364, the canon for schism, alongside Canon 1387 (1382); and the DDF under Pope Leo XIV declared the excommunications of July 2, 2026 on the same ground. Priests ordained by bishops whose consecrations constitute schismatic acts, operating under declared suspension from a body the Holy See has refused canonical recognition in every pontificate since Paul VI, are not tolerated in the ministry. Therefore the faithful who knowingly receive from SSPX ministers communicate in their sin. I answer that The faithful sin by receiving Holy Communion from the hands of SSPX priests, provided they have sufficient knowledge of the canonical situation. Saint Thomas’s principle is clear: the key distinction is between a minister tolerated in his ministry and one the Church does not tolerate by virtue of suspension or excommunication. 11 SSPX priests fall into the latter category — suspended by canonical act, declared by papal authority, and confirmed across every pontificate since Paul VI. It must be acknowledged: no Vatican document explicitly declares 'receiving Communion at an SSPX Mass is a grave sin.' The present conclusion is derived from Aquinas's principle applied to the canonical situation established in Article 1, and it draws its strength from that premise, namely, the Church does not tolerate SSPX as public ministers. The question here is whether Rome's consistent position constitutes non-toleration in precisely Aquinas's sense. The answer is yes, for the following cumulative reasons: (1) Paul VI formally declared Lefebvre suspended a divinis in 1976 — a direct removal from ministry; (2) Benedict XVI explicitly stated that SSPX ministers 'do not legitimately exercise any ministry in the Church' even after lifting the excommunications in 2009; (3) the PCILT warned in 1996 that formal adherence to the Lefebvrian schism carries automatic excommunication. This warning addresses adherence, not mere attendance, but this presupposes the ministry “adhered to” is not tolerated by the Church; (4) Rome has refused to grant canonical status in every pontificate since Paul VI. This is not a contested judgment about a peripheral matter — it is the cumulative, consistent, formally expressed position of the Church over fifty years. The Church does not tolerate SSPX ministers in the ministry in precisely the sense Aquinas requires. That said, the conclusion speaks objectively and does not accuse as mortally sinful every act of reception. Subjective culpability follows knowledge: the visitor who wanders into an SSPX chapel completely unaware of its status does not sin; others with confused or partial knowledge are guilty to the degree of their knowledge; those, however, who know the Church's judgment and knowingly receive are therefore guilty as we have stated above. Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
heading: Reply to Objection 1. Validity Does Not Affect Reception from a Non-Tolerated Minister
text: See Objection The objection correctly states the principle for priests in good canonical standing. However St. Thomas himself distinguishes the two cases in the same article. 12 The priest in good standing celebrates on behalf of the Church — his personal sin is his own. The suspended priest acts on his own authority, having been removed from the Church's ministry. The valid confection of the sacrament and the lawfulness of receiving it are distinct questions. SSPX Masses are valid. The question is not sacramental validity but whether the faithful communicate in the minister's irregularity by knowingly receiving from him. Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
heading: Reply to Objection 2. The Hawaii Five Ruling, Perl Letter, and PCILT Address Schism and Not the Thomistic Principle of Communication in the Sin of the Non-Tolerated Minister
text: See Objection Cardinal Ratzinger's ruling 13 addressed the canonical crime of schism under Canon 1364 14 — not the moral question of whether receiving Communion from a suspended minister involves the faithful in his sin under Aquinas's principle. Canonical crime and sinful actions are not identical categories. The Perl letter warrants the same distinction, and two further ones. First, a private response of the Pontifical Commission is not an authentic interpretation of law and carries no legislative force — a limit the Commission itself insisted upon when it publicly clarified in January 2003 that its September 2002 letter was a private communication addressed to one correspondent's circumstances. Second, the fulfillment of the Sunday obligation under Canon 1248 §1 turns on assistance at a Mass celebrated in a Catholic rite — a question distinct from whether knowingly receiving from a non-tolerated minister is licit, which is this Article's thesis. Indeed the same 2003 clarification stated that SSPX priests are suspended from exercising their priestly functions and that their Masses, though valid, are illicit — the very premise from which this Article argues. The 1996 PCILT clarification 15 further distinguished occasional attendance without the schismatic spirit from habitual exclusive attendance combined with use of SSPX sacramental structures. The Hawaii Five ruling and the Perl letter are properly cited in SSPX-adjacent arguments, and their weight is acknowledged here. Neither, however, addresses the Thomistic principle at stake. Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
heading: Reply to Objection 3. Probable Opinion Does Not Work When the Competent Authority Rules Otherwise
text: See Objection Disagreement among theologians establishes a probable opinion — where the question remains open. But probabilism presupposes a doubt of law that authority has not resolved. Here the legislator himself has spoken on the meaning of his own discipline: Benedict XVI has stated that SSPX ministers do not legitimately exercise any ministry in the Church; the Holy See has refused canonical recognition in every pontificate since Paul VI; and the DDF's decree of July 2, 2026 reaffirmed the schismatic character of the Society's episcopal acts. A private theological opinion, however learned, cannot reopen a question against which the declared judgment of the lawgiver has closed. The theologians cited argue what the law should permit, or what supplied jurisdiction would cover if the conditions held; the competent authority to determine whether those conditions hold has consistently determined they do not. The question is not whether theologians disagree, but whether a probable opinion survives contrary resolution by the legislator. It does not. Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
heading: Reply to Objection 4. Faculties for Confessions and Marriage Benefit the Faithful, Not Determine the Status of the Minister
text: See Objection The Francis provisions supply jurisdiction for the faithful's benefit precisely because the faithful would otherwise be in an uncertain sacramental position. Rome's solution was to ensure validity for the faithful — not to declare SSPX ministry fully licit. 16 The scope of the grants confirms this reading: Rome supplied faculties for precisely the two sacraments in which jurisdiction touches validity — penance and marriage — and granted nothing for the Eucharist, whose validity was never in question. The provisions repair what a defect of jurisdiction would invalidate; they leave the question of liceity exactly where it stood. A second reason is pastoral mercy: Rome does not want the faithful to lose access to sacramental grace due to clerical irregularity they did not create. This is analogous to the emergency provision of Canon 976, which permits even an excommunicated priest to absolve in danger of death — without that being a declaration that the excommunicated priest's ministry is otherwise licit. Finally, the objection’s premise no longer holds. The Explanatory Note of 2 July 2026 declares the sacrament of penance administered by the Society’s priests, and the marriages at which they assist, to be invalid ( Appendix A, A.7 , §3). It is no answer that the Note does not in terms revoke the faculties Pope Francis granted. A faculty is a concession of jurisdiction — exercisable only within the communion it presupposes, and revocable by the authority that grants it — not a possession the minister retains against the Church; and the same Canon 976 just cited shows that excommunication and valid absolution do not coexist outside danger of death. The competent authority has now declared these ministers schismatic and their penance and marriages invalid; that determination negates the very effect the earlier grant existed to produce, without needing to name it, and in the external forum the faithful are bound by it. What the provisions secured while the Society remained within reach of communion is answered above; what they secure now is nothing. Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
heading: Reply to Objection 5. Canon 844 Does Not Apply to the SSPX Situation
text: See Objection The objection conflates two distinct legal categories and stretches a narrow exception past its plain terms. Canon 844 §1 states without qualification that Catholic ministers administer the sacraments licitly only to Catholics, who likewise receive them licitly only from Catholic ministers; §2 is a narrow, conditioned exception, not a general license. All conditions must hold simultaneously: necessity or genuine spiritual advantage; no danger of error or indifferentism; physical or moral impossibility of approaching a Catholic minister; and a minister belonging to a church whose sacraments are valid. The canon’s standard application — Catholics in Siberia or comparable regions with no resident Catholic clergy at all — shows ‘morally impossible’ means the practical absence of any Catholic minister, not the presence of one whose Mass is found liturgically unsatisfying. A licit Novus Ordo Mass, offered by a priest in good standing, is a Catholic minister in the canon’s sense. Its availability extinguishes the §2 exception on its own terms, regardless of one’s assessment of its liturgical merit. Reading ‘moral impossibility’ to include ‘access to a Mass I find spiritually deficient’ would empty the clause of content, since any dissatisfaction could be redescribed as a burden. The canon has never been interpreted by the Holy See to cover liturgical preference. The objection trades on an equivocation latent in the word schismatic . Articles 2 and 3 use it in the sense Canon 751 defines — refusal of submission to the Roman Pontiff by a determinate act, and the personal canonical crime that formal adherence to such an act incurs under Canon 1364. Canon 844 uses ‘non-Catholic minister’ in a structurally different sense: a minister of a church Rome has institutionally recognized as existing in imperfect communion, per Unitatis Redintegratio 3, 17 a determination Canon 844 §3 reserves explicitly to the Apostolic See’s own judgment. The Holy See has made that determination about the Orthodox; it has never made it about the SSPX. Indeed the opposite is true: that Rome subjects SSPX bishops to penalties under the Latin Code at all — most recently in the Decree of 2 July 2026, which grounded the excommunications in Canon 1364 §1 of the Code itself — presupposes they remain subject to that law as Catholics, since merely ecclesiastical law binds only the baptized Catholic (Canon 11). 18 That is a jurisdiction Rome does not claim, and could not coherently claim, over Orthodox clergy. The schismatic character of an act, and the personal canonical crime of formal adherence to it, are categories internal to the Catholic Church’s own discipline. They do not convert the SSPX into a separate ecclesial body of the kind Canon 844 references. The objection stated that if you can receive from the Orthodox, then a fortiori you can receive from the SSPX. But this inference runs backward. The objection argues the SSPX case is easier because its priests affirm the papacy while Orthodox clergy deny it. But Canon 844 §2 does not turn on the minister’s doctrinal soundness — it turns on the petitioner’s access to any Catholic minister at all. The Orthodox exception is not a reward for correct doctrine; it is a narrow accommodation to physical isolation. SSPX doctrinal fidelity, however genuine, is not the variable the canon regulates. The operative distinction in this Article remains Aquinas’s tolerated and non-tolerated minister, not Canon 844. The canon answers whether a Catholic may receive from a minister outside the Church; this Article asks whether a Catholic may receive from a suspended minister inside the Church. It is precisely the second question the objection fails to answer. Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Commit Sin by Receiving Holy Communion from the SSPX.
heading: Reply to Objection 6. Canon 1335 §2 Answers Hidden Censures — the SSPX's Censure Is Public and Declared
text: See Objection This is the strongest objection in the Article, yet it still fails to demonstrate it is morally licit to receive Communion from the SSPX. And this for three reasons: First, the condition the canon protects — a censure hidden from the faithful who approach the minister — is absent here. Paul VI declared the suspension a divinis in 1976; John Paul II declared the excommunications and the schismatic character of the 1988 consecrations in Ecclesia Dei adflicta ; the DDF declared the excommunications of July 2, 2026. Benedict XVI stated in 2009 that the Society's ministers do not legitimately exercise any ministry in the Church. Canon 1335 §2 covers the private censure unknown to the public — the priest under a hidden impediment. Consider a classic example: a priest who violated the seal of confession in a private conversation and incurs latae sententiae excommunication under Canon 1386 §1 (former Canon 1388 §1, on the seal). Suppose immediately after this conversation, he has to celebrate Sunday Mass for his parish. Due to the hidden nature of his censure, Canon 1335 §2 would apply. Nothing exterior would alert the faithful to his canonical status. The SSPX priest's status is not private in this sense. His impediment is the public, declared, and repeatedly reaffirmed position of the Apostolic See toward the body whose ministry he exercises. To apply a provision designed for hidden censures to a ministry whose irregularity has been proclaimed by six popes is to read the letter against the mind of the legislator (Canon 17). Nor does the strict reading of the July 2026 Decree change this. Grant for the sake of argument everything the objection draws from Canon 18: that the Decree declares censures for six bishops alone, and that its conditional warning to clerics and faithful concedes the excommunication of Canon 1364 has not yet fallen on them. All of this touches on the question of the excommunication — a question this Article's argument does not need to win. The argument rests on the suspension established in Article 1 and on the non-toleration of the Society's ministry, declared in 1976 and restated in 2009 — a standing condition the 2026 Decree presupposes rather than creates. Read as strictly as the objection wishes, the Decree leaves the Aquinas premise exactly where it has stood for fifty years; and its Nota Esplicativa, far from retreating, states the ministers' condition in the present tense: non-tolerated. Second, the canon's own rationale confines it to a concrete act, not a standing arrangement. A provision protecting the faithful's access to sacraments in the moment of request cannot justify a permanent parallel sacramental system. The scale the "request" reaches is no longer one of the faithful approaching a minister, but the faithful collectively sustaining the very ministry the censure exists to suppress — the exception swallowing the rule it qualifies. The just cause the canon recognizes in the individual request does not become permission for the habitual scheduling of “requests” on a chapel calendar, any more than Canon 976's emergency absolution licenses an excommunicated priest to open a confessional. 19 And where reception becomes habitual and structural, a distinct consideration enters: the 1996 PCILT Note — which addresses not this canon but the conditions of adherence — warned that making the Society's structures one's stable provision shades into the formal adherence that carries its own canonical consequence. The objection is correct: the Note is not an authentic interpretation under Canon 16 §1, and nothing here rests on its legislative force. It is invoked for what it articulates, not what it enacts: the distinction between occasional attendance and formal adherence flows from Canons 751 and 1364 themselves, and the Note's statement of it has now been expressly adopted by the DDF's Nota Esplicativa of July 2, 2026, which declares the Note "still in force" and makes its conditions the Dicastery's own. 20 The Note may lack the force of law and yet remain the authoritative statement of how the legislator understands his own law. This is precisely the standing this Article's argument requires of it, and no more. Third, and decisively, the canon suspends the minister's prohibition ; it does not settle the faithful's cooperation . Canon 1335 §2 answers whether the censured priest violates his censure by ministering when asked — a question about his juridical position, enacted for the faithful's benefit. It does not touch the prior moral question of what the requester does. Nor does the suspension of the prohibition legitimate the ministry: the canon removes the minister’s delict in the act of ministering when asked; it confers neither faculties nor mission, and the exercise remains illegitimate though unpunished. Requesting a sacrament from a non-tolerated minister is, at minimum, material cooperation in the illegitimate exercise of his ministry. Where the reception is habitual and the Society's chapel becomes one's stable provision, the cooperation becomes proximate, sustains the irregular ministry itself, and adds scandal. When joined to adherence to the Society's position against the Apostolic See, it becomes formal, and the faithful communicate in the minister's sin in exactly the sense Aquinas describes. page: Whether the Faithful Sin by Merely Attending the Mass of the SSPX Without Receiving Holy Communion.
text: Article 6 Whether the Faithful Sin by Merely Attending the Mass of the SSPX Without Receiving Holy Communion.

page: Whether the Faithful Sin by Merely Attending the Mass of the SSPX Without Receiving Holy Communion.
heading: Objection 1. St. Thomas Speaks of Receiving a Sacrament, Not Mere Attendance
text: Saint Thomas's principle at Q.64 A.6 1 specifies that one who receives a sacrament from a suspended minister sins. Mere physical attendance at Mass without sacramental reception does not constitute receiving a sacrament from the minister. Thomas's language does not extend to presence without reception. Therefore mere attendance without reception does not fall under Thomas's condemnation. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Sin by Merely Attending the Mass of the SSPX Without Receiving Holy Communion.
heading: Objection 2. Occasional Attendance Is Distinct from Formal Adherence
text: Furthermore the 1996 PCILT clarification 2 explicitly distinguished occasional attendance without the schismatic spirit from formal adherence to schism. If Rome itself draws this distinction, occasional attendance without reception cannot constitute the kind of sin that would require avoidance altogether. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Sin by Merely Attending the Mass of the SSPX Without Receiving Holy Communion.
heading: Objection 3. Ecclesia Dei Permitted Fulfillment of Your Sunday Obligation
text: Furthermore, the Holy See itself has repeatedly affirmed that the faithful may fulfill the Sunday obligation at an SSPX Mass. Msgr. Camille Perl, Secretary of the Pontifical Commission Ecclesia Dei, wrote in 1996, and repeated in 1998, that "in the strict sense you may fulfill your Sunday obligation by attending a Mass celebrated by a priest of the Society of Saint Pius X," 3 and the Commission stated publicly in 2003 that one who attends simply to participate in a Mass according to the 1962 Missal for the sake of devotion does not sin, adding that a modest contribution to the collection could be justified. 4 But an act by which a grave precept of the Church is satisfied cannot at the same time be a sin against her. Therefore attendance without reception is licit. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Sin by Merely Attending the Mass of the SSPX Without Receiving Holy Communion.
heading: On the contrary, (DDF Explicitly Tells Faithful to Abstain From Attendance, Not Just Reception of Holy Communion)
text: The Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith, in its Explanatory Note of 2 July 2026, exhorts all the faithful "to abstain from participating in the celebrations and activities promoted by the aforementioned Priestly Fraternity of Saint Pius X." 5 The Apostolic See here addresses not the reception of the sacraments but participation itself. I answer that Saint Thomas states the governing principle, Summa Theologiae III, Q.82, A.9: 'Heretical, schismatical, excommunicate, or even sinful priests, although they have the power to consecrate the Eucharist, yet they do not make a proper use of it; on the contrary, they sin by using it. But whoever communicates with another who is in sin, becomes a sharer in his sin. Consequently, it is not lawful to receive Communion from them, or to assist at their Mass. Still there is a difference among the above, because heretics, schismatics, and excommunicates, have been forbidden, by the Church's sentence, to perform the Eucharistic rite. And therefore whoever hears their Mass or receives the sacraments from them, commits sin.' Mere attendance at an SSPX Mass without reception of Holy Communion occupies a genuinely grey area — one that intellectual honesty requires acknowledging directly. Saint Thomas's precise language — 'receives a sacrament from him' — does not obviously extend to mere physical presence without sacramental reception. This objection is valid and is not fully resolved by the primary argument. However four practical qualifications are necessary. First, the theoretical grey area does not describe the actual situation of most of the faithful addressed. Whatever the older discipline of infrequent communion, the faithful who make an SSPX chapel their regular provision today do not, as a class, permanently abstain from its communion rail. In practice, habitual SSPX attendance means habitual reception from suspended ministers. Second, even where reception is absent, habitual exclusive attendance at SSPX structures — combined with use of their confessional, their marriage tribunal, 6 their schools, and their publications — constitutes evidence of formal adherence under the criteria identified by the PCILT, though formal adherence in the canonical sense also requires the internal element of agreement with the substance of the schism. 7 Third, if reception of Holy Communion from these ministers is gravely illicit — as the preceding Article concludes — then habitual attendance at their Masses constitutes a voluntary proximate occasion of that sin. The moral theologians teach that one who freely and without proportionate cause places himself in the proximate occasion of grave sin thereby sins gravely by that very fact. 8 Of the classical categories of occasion — persons, places, and things — all three converge in the weekly frequentation of an SSPX chapel: the minister who will offer Communion, the place ordered to its distribution, and the sacred action that invites it. Nor is presence the only cooperation involved: attendance swells the congregation whose numbers the Society cites in its own vindication, and contribution to the collection materially sustains a ministry now under declared censure. Fourth, while in ordinary doubts of law the faithful may follow a solidly probable opinion, the received moral theology binds them to the safer course where the danger of grave sin is proximate and voluntary — and that, by the third point above, is precisely the case here. In dubio, tutior pars eligenda applies not as a general rule of conduct but in its uncontested zone: where what is risked is not inconvenience but grave sin itself 9 . Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Sin by Merely Attending the Mass of the SSPX Without Receiving Holy Communion.
heading: Reply to Objection 1. Thomas's Language Is Granted — but the Voluntary Occasion of Sin Remains
text: See Objection This objection is granted as far as it goes. Thomas's language does not clearly condemn mere attendance without reception, and the grey area is genuinely acknowledged. That concession, however, is owed only to the passage the objection cites; Saint Thomas speaks to attendance in its own right, Summa Theologiae III, Q.82, A.9: 'Heretical, schismatical, excommunicate, or even sinful priests, although they have the power to consecrate the Eucharist, yet they do not make a proper use of it; on the contrary, they sin by using it. But whoever communicates with another who is in sin, becomes a sharer in his sin. Consequently, it is not lawful to receive Communion from them, or to assist at their Mass. Still there is a difference among the above, because heretics, schismatics, and excommunicates, have been forbidden, by the Church's sentence, to perform the Eucharistic rite. And therefore whoever hears their Mass or receives the sacraments from them, commits sin.' His words reach the hearing of their Mass, not the reception of a sacrament alone. The practical argument above — that habitual attendance rarely exists without reception — addresses the pastoral situation without resolving the theoretical question. What escapes the grey area entirely, however, is the question of occasion: one who grants that reception is gravely illicit cannot treat weekly presence at its offering as morally neutral, for the reasons given in the body of the Article. Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Sin by Merely Attending the Mass of the SSPX Without Receiving Holy Communion.
heading: Reply to Objection 2. PCILT Distinction Regards Schism Specifically and Does Not Answer Moral Liceity of Attendance
text: See Objection The PCILT distinction between occasional attendance and formal adherence 10 is correctly cited and its weight acknowledged. That distinction was made in the context of the canonical crime of schism, not the full moral question attending SSPX participation. It establishes a floor below which canonical schism is not incurred — not a ceiling above which no moral concern arises. Moreover, the faithful who attend habitually are surrounded by those who formally adhere and are formed week by week by preaching that defends the Society's positions; in such an environment, resistance to formal adherence grows steadily more difficult. What was said in the body of the Article concerning the voluntary occasion of sin applies to this danger as well. Return to top

page: Whether the Faithful Sin by Merely Attending the Mass of the SSPX Without Receiving Holy Communion.
heading: Reply to Objection 3. Toleration Has Been Formally Withdrawn — Canonical Sufficiency Was Never Moral Permission
text: See Objection This objection states the strongest case for attendance, and its sources are genuine; yet its arguments do not hold. Three things must be said. First, every one of these responses belonged to a posture of toleration that has now been formally terminated. The Ecclesia Dei Commission was created precisely to accompany the faithful attached to the Society back toward full communion, and its letters were issued while Rome assumed a more lenient posture toward the Society's ministers, in the hope that they would respond in kind and reconcile. That toleration, moreover, was always directed at the faithful rather than the ministers: the Commission's responses addressed whether attendance was sinful for the one attending, while the priests themselves remained suspended throughout, without faculties, their ministry never approved. Saint Thomas's principle hinges on this: one may receive from — a fortiori assist at the Mass of — a minister whom the Church tolerates in the ministry. The Decree of 2 July 2026 removes the hinge. The same Apostolic See that once wrote that it could not recommend attendance now exhorts the faithful to abstain from participation altogether. A dicasterial courtesy of 2003 cannot survive the contrary dicasterial acts of 2026; the later and more authoritative acts govern. Second, the objection collapses canonical sufficiency into moral permission. Even granting — as this document itself argues — that the Society's ministers remain Catholics under the Church's internal discipline, so that their Masses may satisfy the letter of Canon 1248 §1, 11 the question whether an act satisfies the precept is distinct from the question whether the act is licit. The precept obliges assistance at Mass; it does not permit assistance at any Mass regardless of circumstances or canonical situation. The Commission's own 2003 letter 12 embodies the distinction the objection ignores: in the same document it stated that while the obligation could "in the strict sense" be fulfilled, it nonetheless could not recommend attendance, and warned that attendance with a schismatic intention is sinful. What was already a discouraged sufficiency under toleration cannot become a positive permission after toleration has been withdrawn. Third, the same reasoning governs the collection. What was a justifiable modest contribution toward tolerated ministers is, after 2 July 2026, material support of a ministry the Apostolic See has directed the faithful to avoid — a cooperation treated in the body of this Article.
