page: Whether Arguments From Ecclesial Inconsistency, From the Crisis in the Church, and From the Sincerity and Suffering of the Faithful Bear Upon the Liceity of Receiving Holy Communion From the SSPX.
text: Article 7 Whether Arguments From Ecclesial Inconsistency, From the Crisis in the Church, and From the Sincerity and Suffering of the Faithful Bear Upon the Liceity of Receiving Holy Communion From the SSPX.

page: Whether Arguments From Ecclesial Inconsistency, From the Crisis in the Church, and From the Sincerity and Suffering of the Faithful Bear Upon the Liceity of Receiving Holy Communion From the SSPX.
heading: Objection 1. The Anglican Comparison: Fraternal Prayer with Canterbury vs. Excommunication of Traditional Priests
text: The severity of the sanctions is difficult to reconcile with Rome’s conduct toward those genuinely outside the Church. On 27 April 2026, some nine weeks before the Society was excommunicated, Pope Leo XIV received and prayed with Sarah Mullally, the first woman to hold the See of Canterbury 1 — a minister of a communion the Catholic Church holds to lack valid orders altogether ( Apostolicae Curae ; Dominus Iesus ). If the Roman Pontiff extends fraternal prayer to one who ordains women and blesses same-sex unions, while excommunicating priests whose offense is fidelity to the Church’s perennial faith and liturgy, the sanction cannot be an even-handed act of justice; it betrays an animus against Tradition rather than a concern for unity. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether Arguments From Ecclesial Inconsistency, From the Crisis in the Church, and From the Sincerity and Suffering of the Faithful Bear Upon the Liceity of Receiving Holy Communion From the SSPX.
heading: Objection 2. The Synodal Way Comparison: Selective Discipline and Unpunished Dissent
text: Furthermore, the same disparity appears within the Church. The German “Synodal Way” has advanced positions on sexual morality, the priesthood, and Church governance in open tension with settled teaching, and Fiducia Supplicans (2023) authorized blessings for couples in irregular unions; 2 yet no comparable canonical sanction has fallen on their proponents. When doctrinal dissent at the Church’s center is met with accompaniment, and only traditional Catholics at its margins are met with excommunication, the faithful reasonably conclude that the discipline is selective, and so not binding in conscience as an act of justice. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether Arguments From Ecclesial Inconsistency, From the Crisis in the Church, and From the Sincerity and Suffering of the Faithful Bear Upon the Liceity of Receiving Holy Communion From the SSPX.
heading: Objection 3. The Pachamama Objection: The Moral Authority of the Sanctioning Prelates
text: Furthermore, the moral authority of those imposing the penalty is itself in question. The faithful recall the veneration of the “Pachamama” images during the 2019 Amazon Synod 3 and ask, in the phrase now common among them, “excommunicated by whom?” A censure issued by prelates who have themselves tolerated what the faithful regard as idolatry or grave doctrinal confusion cannot bind the conscience of one who keeps the whole of the Catholic faith. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether Arguments From Ecclesial Inconsistency, From the Crisis in the Church, and From the Sincerity and Suffering of the Faithful Bear Upon the Liceity of Receiving Holy Communion From the SSPX.
heading: Objection 4. "Instructed and Confirmed": The Society's Plea and the Maxim That an Unjust Law Is No Law
text: Furthermore, the Society asked, in its own words, to be “instructed and confirmed in the faith of all time,” and was “declared schismatic a second time.” 4 It sought bread and was given a stone. A penalty that falls upon men for their fidelity, and that the Society itself judges “objectively unjust and invalid,” cannot oblige the faithful, for an unjust law is no law. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether Arguments From Ecclesial Inconsistency, From the Crisis in the Church, and From the Sincerity and Suffering of the Faithful Bear Upon the Liceity of Receiving Holy Communion From the SSPX.
heading: Objection 5. Four Decades of Negotiation: Schism as a Label Rome Manufactured
text: Furthermore, the charge of schism has never truly held. Rome negotiated with the Society for four decades, Pope Benedict XVI remitted the 1988 excommunications in 2009, and Pope Francis granted its priests faculties for confession and marriage. A body with which the Apostolic See prays, negotiates, and shares faculties is not a separated sect; the renewed schism label is a rupture Rome has manufactured, not one the Society created. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether Arguments From Ecclesial Inconsistency, From the Crisis in the Church, and From the Sincerity and Suffering of the Faithful Bear Upon the Liceity of Receiving Holy Communion From the SSPX.
heading: Objection 6. Necessary to Preserve the Faith in an Unprecedented Crisis
text: Furthermore, the Church herself is in a crisis without precedent, and the Society exists to preserve the traditional faith through it. Its Profession of Catholic Faith of 24 June 2026 — one hundred fifty-four paragraphs addressed to Leo XIV and annotated throughout from the Church's own magisterium — professes the whole of Catholic doctrine, 5 and a bishop in full communion has publicly affirmed that the Society does ‘nothing else as to teach and proclaim the same faith as did the saints until the council.’ 6 Its Superior General teaches that one belongs to the Church above all through the integral profession of the faith, and that no one can be made to choose between the Faith and the Church. 7 If modernist error has penetrated even the Church's highest levels, then to assist at the Society's Masses is not to leave the Church but to remain with her tradition: the faithful who kneel at its altars are not communicating in schism but resisting error, and preserving the faith of their baptism where alone it is preached without compromise. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether Arguments From Ecclesial Inconsistency, From the Crisis in the Church, and From the Sincerity and Suffering of the Faithful Bear Upon the Liceity of Receiving Holy Communion From the SSPX.
heading: Objection 7. Without the Consecrations, We Would Lose the Traditional Latin Mass
text: Finally, without the Society's episcopal consecrations the Traditional Latin Mass would have perished, and the faithful would have been left with only the Novus Ordo Missae. It was Archbishop Lefebvre who kept the ancient liturgy alive when it had all but vanished from the Church's altars, and it is the Society's continued existence that restrains Rome from suppressing it altogether. The faithful who assist at the Society's Masses are therefore not communicating in disobedience but safeguarding a treasure of the Church; had they waited upon permissions, there would be nothing left to permit. See Reply Return to top

page: Whether Arguments From Ecclesial Inconsistency, From the Crisis in the Church, and From the Sincerity and Suffering of the Faithful Bear Upon the Liceity of Receiving Holy Communion From the SSPX.
heading: On the contrary, (The Donatist Principle: Validity Does Not Depend on the Worthiness of the Minister)
text: The lawfulness of an individual’s reception of a sacrament is determined by the objective character of the minister’s act and by his communion with the Church, not by the consistency, the motives, or the personal holiness of those who govern. Saint Augustine settled the principle against the Donatists: the validity and the effects of the Church’s acts do not depend upon the worthiness of the minister. 8 And the Council of Constance condemned the proposition of Jan Hus that a prelate in mortal sin does not truly govern — fixing for jurisdiction the same principle Augustine had fixed for the sacraments. 9 I answer that These arguments have real rhetorical force, and honesty requires conceding the truth in them: the Church's discipline is not applied with perfect consistency, and that unevenness is a genuine wound and a scandal to many sincere souls. But not one of them reaches the question the faithful actually face. The first four are either an argument that others sin worse unpunished (the tu quoque ), or an argument that the men who govern are unworthy to govern (the Donatist error), or an appeal to the sincerity and suffering of those sanctioned (a question of imputability, not of liceity). The fifth mistakes forbearance for toleration, and mistakes provisions since revoked for a standing recognition of ministry. The sixth confuses the bond of faith with the bonds of communion, as though material orthodoxy could supply for hierarchical submission. The seventh argues from consequences, as though a good preserved could make licit the means of preserving it. None of them touches the single point on which reception turns: whether the minister is one the Church tolerates in his ministry ( Article 5 ). The inconsistency of a physician does not make a poison a medicine. Return to top

page: Whether Arguments From Ecclesial Inconsistency, From the Crisis in the Church, and From the Sincerity and Suffering of the Faithful Bear Upon the Liceity of Receiving Holy Communion From the SSPX.
heading: Reply to Objection 1. Two Registers: Ecumenism Toward the Separated, Governance Toward the Suspended
text: See Objection The comparison is a category error — the very one dismantled in Article 5, Reply to Objection 5 . Prayer with the Archbishop of Canterbury belongs to the Church’s ecumenical dealings with a separated communion, whose members the Holy See does not treat as Catholics subject to its discipline (Canon 11); the Pope prayed with Mullally precisely as with one outside full communion. The Society’s bishops, by contrast, are Catholics whom Rome disciplines under its own Code. To receive fraternally one who is outside, and to discipline one who is inside, is not a double standard but the difference between two registers — ecumenism and internal governance. The Anglican comparison, far from helping the objection, confirms the distinction on which this work rests: the Society is not a separated church, but suspended Catholic clergy. Return to top

page: Whether Arguments From Ecclesial Inconsistency, From the Crisis in the Church, and From the Sincerity and Suffering of the Faithful Bear Upon the Liceity of Receiving Holy Communion From the SSPX.
heading: Reply to Objection 2. The tu quoque Fallacy: Others' Unpunished Sin Confers No License
text: See Objection The unevenness is real, and the grievance is not baseless; that dissent at the Church’s center often escapes sanction is a scandal this work does not minimize. But “others sin worse and are not punished” has never, in Catholic moral theology, made one’s own act licit. The tu quoque is a fallacy in logic and, dressed in pastoral clothing, remains one in morals: Rome’s failure to discipline the Synodal Way confers on no one a right to communicate in the act of a suspended minister. If anything the argument proves too much, for it would permit any crime by pointing to a worse one left unpunished. Return to top

page: Whether Arguments From Ecclesial Inconsistency, From the Crisis in the Church, and From the Sincerity and Suffering of the Faithful Bear Upon the Liceity of Receiving Holy Communion From the SSPX.
heading: Reply to Objection 3. Donatism Applied to Governance
text: See Objection That the worthiness of the one imposing a penalty does not determine its binding force is not a matter of opinion but of settled doctrine. To hold that a censure binds only if issued by the holy is the Donatist error carried into governance — and on governance the Church has spoken as plainly as on the sacraments, condemning at Constance the proposition that a prelate in mortal sin does not truly rule. The efficacy of the Church's juridical and sacramental acts does not depend on the sanctity of the minister. Whatever the force of the "Pachamama" complaint as a lament over the state of the Church, as an argument against the binding force of a canonical penalty it is the error of Wyclif and Hus applied anew, and it fails as it failed at Constance. heading: Reply to Objection 4. Imputability Is Not Liceity
text: See Objection The sincerity of the Society and the real suffering of its faithful are not in doubt, and they matter — but they bear on imputability, which Article 1 has already treated, not on liceity. A subjectively sincere act can be objectively illicit; a penalty can bind even where the one who incurs it believes himself faithful. That the Society judges the sanction "unjust and invalid" is its own assertion, answered in Article 4; no man is judge in his own cause, and the Church's penal decrees enjoy the presumption of validity until the competent authority, upon lawful recourse, sets them aside (Canon 124 §2). Even an unjust but not null censure is to be feared until it is lawfully annulled ( Article 4, Reply to Objection 2 ). Return to top

heading: Reply to Objection 5. Forbearance Is Not the Absence of the Delict
text: See Objection A father who pleads for decades with a departing son does not thereby prove the son has not left. Rome's long patience, the remission of 2009, and the grant of faculties show forbearance and pastoral mercy, not the absence of the delict. The faculties themselves were framed as extraordinary provisions granted for the pastoral benefit of the faithful, not as a recognition of licit ministry; 10 and the acts of 2 July 2026 have declared that “the sacrament of penance administered by them and the marriage assisted by them are invalid,” 11 so that the datum on which the objection chiefly rests no longer exists. Indeed the Decree of 2 July 2026 carries the very protocol number of the 2009 remission, marking that act of mercy as one the renewed consecrations have now undone. The Society's separation is not a label Rome invented in 2026; it is the canonical situation the Society created in 1988, was offered the means to heal in the Protocol it first signed and then abandoned, and has now renewed at Écône. heading: Reply to Objection 6. Orthodoxy Alone Does Not Excuse Schism
text: See Objection The crisis is real, and this work has not denied it; nor is the Profession's orthodoxy in question across the great body of its paragraphs — its doctrine of God, of Christ, of Our Lady, of the sacraments and the last things is the doctrine of Trent and of Vatican I, cited from the Church's own sources. Indeed the document is itself evidence for the central contention of this work: no separated confession composes a profession of faith annotated from Denzinger and addresses it to the Roman Pontiff. Men who write to the Pope as their father are Catholics under his discipline, not ministers of another church ( Article 5 ). But schism, in the Church's own definition, is not heterodoxy; it is "the refusal of submission to the Supreme Pontiff or of communion with the members of the Church subject to him" (Canon 751). A man may hold every article of the Creed and still refuse that submission, and the Profession documents the refusal with the same candor with which it documents the orthodoxy. It rejects the Council's teaching on religious liberty in itself, and not merely its abuses; it negates, nearly word for word, the Council's teaching on the imperfect communion of separated Christians and on the rays of truth in the non-Christian religions; it rejects the received doctrine on the episcopal college and the legitimacy of the liturgical reform. 12 And beneath these particular rejections lies the formal one: the Profession reserves to the professing subject the judgment of which acts of the living Magisterium bind, measuring the present teaching office against the past as the Society reads it. This is to displace the proximate rule of faith from the living Magisterium, where the Church has fixed it, to a private adjudication of the prior Magisterium — the very hermeneutic of rupture Benedict XVI identified in 2005, which is shared, as he observed, by those who welcome the rupture and by those who deplore it. 13 The Profession professes that it judges no one and usurps no authority; its own articles do the judging. 14 Nor is the Superior General's teaching — that one belongs to the Church above all by the profession of the integral faith — the traditional doctrine it claims to be. Pius XII teaches that those really belong to the Church who are baptized, profess the true faith, and have neither separated themselves from the unity of the Body nor been excluded by legitimate authority: 15 the bond of faith is one bond of three, and the faith itself includes what the Church has always taught about her own visible, hierarchical communion ( Article 2 ). To invoke the integral faith against the requirement of communion is therefore not to escape the dilemma but to profess the faith minus one of its articles. Even were everything the objection says of the crisis granted, the conclusion would not follow, for orthodoxy confers no faculties. Suspension falls upon the orthodox and the heterodox alike; the question on which reception turns — whether the Church tolerates the minister in his ministry ( Article 5 ) — is not altered by the soundness of the minister's preaching. The faithful who would resist error must resist it as Catholics have always resisted it: within the Church's communion, not at altars she has forbidden. To preserve the traditional faith by means the traditional faith condemns is not preservation; it is the crisis, entered rather than resisted. Return to top

heading: Reply to Objection 7. The Traditional Latin Mass Is Preserved by Fidelity and Unity, Not Disobedience
text: See Objection Archbishop Lefebvre's movement was a real factor in the survival of the Traditional Latin Mass. But the objection rests on a counterfactual asserted as fact, and counterfactuals cut both ways: we cannot know what would have happened without the Society — nor how the ancient rite would have flourished under bishops with full pontifical mandate, had the Protocol of 1988 been upheld. 16 What we do know is what did happen. The rite was never abolished, and permission for it, restricted but real, has never lapsed: the English indult of 1971, the indult of 1984, the Ecclesia Dei adflicta communities of 1988, the declaration of 2007 that the old Missal was never abrogated, and even the restrictions of 2021, which curtailed but did not suppress — while in 2022 the Holy See expressly confirmed the Priestly Fraternity of Saint Peter in its proper liturgical books. 17 Extinction has been prophesied at every turn; at every turn it has remained a prophecy. Indeed the objection nearly inverts the causality. When Rome did restrict the ancient rite, the stated reason was not the rite itself but its use as a banner of division and of rejection of the Council. That association — the Traditional Mass with disobedience — is the Society's most enduring contribution to the question, 18 and the communities in full communion have carried it as a burden ever since, obliged to persuade their bishops that love of the ancient liturgy is not a badge of schism. 19 If the Traditional Mass has enemies, nothing has armed them so well as its defense by illicit means. Obedience within the hierarchy is harder than independence. Operating in the Church requires patience, negotiation, and submission that autonomy avoids entirely. The easier path does not make it the correct one any more than the difficulty of the straight and narrow prevents it from being the way to Heaven. And even if the Society's disobedience did win concessions, it is never lawful to do evil that good may come (Romans 3:8); that God draws good from evil is the Creator's prerogative, not the creature's license. The deeper reason lies in the thing defended. The Eucharist is, in Augustine's words, the sign of unity and the bond of charity; 20 a sacrament of unity is not preserved by rupturing unity — that is to save the sign by destroying what it signifies. Israel received the Temple and the Land and lost both, not because God's gifts fail, but through infidelity to the Giver; the prophet's warning fell precisely on those who cried "the temple of the Lord, the temple of the Lord" while refusing obedience (Jeremiah 7:4). If the ancient liturgy is pleasing to God — and the Church herself calls it venerable 21 — it will not be lost except as the Temple was lost. It is kept as every gift of God is kept: by fidelity and obedience within the household of the faith. Return to top ← Article 6 Contents Conclusion →

page: Conclusion
text: Conclusion Subject to the premises established in Article 1 — that SSPX priests are suspended by canonical act and papal declaration, and that the Church therefore does not tolerate them in the ministry in the sense Aquinas requires — the faithful who knowingly receive Holy Communion from SSPX priests commit sin. They participate in the sin of the ministers whom the Church no longer tolerates to act in her name. See Summa Theologiae III, Q.64, A.6, Reply to Objection 2. 1 Membership in the Catholic Church requires the three bonds of unity: 2 same Sacraments, same Faith, and same Hierarchy. Habitual participation in the sacramental life of a body operating outside legitimate hierarchical authority endangers the third bond in ways the faithful should not lightly risk. Saint Thomas marks the further consequence for the fruit of such a Mass: the severed priest still confects the sacrament validly, yet the prayers he offers in the Church's name are without efficacy, Summa Theologiae III, Q.82, A.7, Reply to Objection 3: 'The priest, in reciting the prayers of the Mass, speaks instead of the Church, in whose unity he remains; but in consecrating the sacrament he speaks as in the person of Christ, Whose place he holds by the power of his orders. Consequently, if a priest severed from the unity of the Church celebrates Mass, not having lost the power of order, he consecrates Christ's true body and blood; but because he is severed from the unity of the Church, his prayers have no efficacy.' It is important to state clearly what this conclusion does and does not claim. It does not claim that a Vatican document explicitly declares such reception a grave sin — no such document has been located. The conclusion is derived from applying Aquinas's principle to the canonical situation established in Article 1. The strength of the conclusion therefore depends on the suspension argument established in Article 1: that SSPX priests are suspended by canonical act and papal declaration, and that this constitutes non-toleration in the ministerial sense Aquinas requires. Those who hold that the SSPX necessity argument successfully defeats the suspension — and the Society's canonists have pressed it (see Article 1, Objection 4 , and Article 3, Objection 3 ) — will not be moved by the present conclusion. The purpose of this document is not to resolve that underlying canonical dispute but to demonstrate that it has serious moral consequences that cannot be set aside. The consecration of four new SSPX bishops on July 1, 2026 — carried out despite the Holy See’s explicit warning that it would be a schismatic act 3 carrying automatic excommunication 4 , and despite Pope Leo XIV’s personal appeal of June 29 — has deepened rather than resolved the canonical irregularity on which this conclusion rests. On July 2, 2026, the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith declared that the consecrating and consecrated bishops had incurred latae sententiae excommunication, and that the Society’s clergy and formally adhering faithful are in schism. This second generation of illicit episcopal consecrations mirrors 1988 in every essential respect 5 and forecloses the argument that the SSPX situation is trending toward normalization. Nor are the episcopal norms violated here a novelty of the postconciliar Church — they predate the Second Vatican Council by centuries: from the fourth canon of Nicaea through Trent, from Pius VI's Charitas through the Code of Pope Saint Pius X to the present law, the requirement that no man be made a bishop against the will of the Church's supreme authority spans the whole of the tradition the Society claims to defend (see Article 2, Reply to Objection 4 ). A further observation emerges from the FSSP's founding theological essay. 6 The SSPX's stated position is that the Pope is legitimate, ordinary obedience is required, but extraordinary necessity justifies resistance in the present crisis. This position deserves to be stated in its strongest form before being examined. The SSPX does not claim to be sedevacantist. It claims to recognize papal authority in principle while acting outside its exercise in practice, on grounds of necessity. This is a coherent statement of intent. The question is whether it is coherent in execution. The FSSP authors identify the tension: if the reigning Pope possesses full and legitimate authority — which the SSPX affirms — then his explicit, personal, repeated prohibition of the 1988 consecrations was a legitimate exercise of that authority. To act against it on grounds of 'necessity' while affirming his legitimacy requires showing that the necessity was genuine and that no legitimate means were available. The Protocol of Accord of 1988, signed by Lefebvre and repudiated the following morning, shows legitimate means were available and refused. The July 1, 2026 consecrations, proceeding after Leo XIV's personal appeal and Rome's formal offer of dialogue, repeat this pattern. At each point where the necessity argument might have been validated by the absence of alternatives, an alternative was offered and declined. The SSPX's position is not incoherent in principle; it is defeated in fact by the historical record of refused solutions. A note on the language of this document. Some will observe that the Catholic Church today generally avoids the words schism and schismatic in its official dealings with separated communities. The Orthodox are not called schismatics in modern Vatican documents. Protestants are not called heretics. The Second Vatican Council's own Decree on Ecumenism adopted the language of separated brethren for these communities, and that gentler vocabulary has shaped official usage since. 7 The SSPX invokes this modern pastoral restraint to argue that applying schism language to them is anachronistic, aggressive, or indicative of a pre-conciliar mentality. This argument, while accurately describing modern practice, actually cuts against the SSPX rather than for it. The Council's own decree on ecumenism supplies the reason for the gentler vocabulary, and that reason is fatal to the Society's use of it: 'The children who are born into these Communities and who grow up believing in Christ cannot be accused of the sin involved in the separation' ( Unitatis Redintegratio 3). 8 The softened language reflects the Church's recognition that those born and raised in separated communities did not personally commit the act of separation — they cannot be charged with willfully breaking communion. The SSPX has no such defense. Archbishop Lefebvre was a bishop of the Catholic Church in full communion, who was personally warned, who signed and repudiated an accord, and who proceeded against explicit papal prohibition. This is not the inherited situation of an Orthodox Christian or a cradle Protestant. It is a fresh act of separation by someone who knew exactly what he was doing and was told explicitly by the Pope what the consequences would be. Furthermore, the language of schism and schismatic act was not imposed on the SSPX by traditionalist critics speaking anachronistically. It was applied to them in 1988 by the Pope himself in real time. Pope John Paul II used the precise word schismatic in Ecclesia Dei adflicta on July 2, 1988 — two days after the consecrations. Cardinal Gantin's decree of excommunication cited Canon 1364, 9 the canon for schism, alongside Canon 1387 (1382). The SSPX was thus not called schismatic by general retrospective labeling: they were called schismatic by the formal canonical act that created their situation, by the Pope whose legitimacy they simultaneously affirm. Leo XIV's Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith used identical language in May 2026. The word has been consistent throughout. What has changed is not the Church's canonical assessment but the SSPX's hope that modern pastoral language might obscure it. The reactivation of this language in 2026 is not a reversion to pre-conciliar harshness. It is the application of the traditional doctrine of medicinal penalty to a situation that nearly four decades of accompaniment and dialogue have not resolved. 10 Canon law is explicit: censures including excommunication are medicinal in intent — imposed not to punish vindictively but to bring the offender to repentance and restoration of communion. To accompany indefinitely without correction is not mercy — it is the ratification of a canonical situation the Church has consistently identified as harmful to those within it and to the unity of the Body of Christ. The purpose of calling the act what it is — schismatic — and applying the penalty the law provides, is precisely the restoration of those who have departed, not their condemnation. It is worth noting that the SSPX argues the faithful should avoid Novus Ordo Masses on the basis of possible theological deficiency and probable spiritual danger — a standard based on possibility and inference. Those who accept this probabilistic standard have all the more reason to take seriously the present argument, which rests on documented canonical fact rather than probability. 11 The faithful are invited to examine the sources cited in the footnotes and to verify every claim independently. All canonical texts, papal documents, and primary Aquinas texts are freely available at the links provided. ← Article 7 Contents Appendix A →

English rendering via Vatican News. To the Reverend Fr. Davide Pagliarani Superior General of the Priestly Fraternity of Saint Pius X With a paternal heart, and aware of the responsibility entrusted to me by the Lord as the Successor of the Apostle Peter, I address you and, through you, the bishops, priests, seminarians and faithful connected to the Priestly Fraternity of Saint Pius X. The Church recognizes the devotion to liturgical life, commitment to priestly formation, apostolic zeal and desire for fidelity to Tradition that characterize many people and communities connected to your Fraternity. This has motivated the attentive and generous attitude that my Predecessors have consistently shown to you. In this spirit, and filled with Christian affection, I plead with you and ask you with all my heart: please turn back! I urge you to consider carefully the spiritual good of the faithful, because the schismatic act you are about to undertake would deprive them of the licit and, in some cases, even valid reception of the Sacraments, which they love and seek for their sanctification. The Church is open to a path of dialogue and understanding that the Holy Spirit can make possible and fruitful. I pray for you, because to tear the seamless garment of Christ is a sin of extreme gravity. May the Lord enlighten your consciences and awaken your hearts. With a sorrowful yet hopeful heart, I feel it is my duty, through the authority received from Christ, to ask you to desist from your intended act. I entrust these intentions to the Immaculate Heart of Mary, Mother of Good Counsel. From the Vatican, 29 June 2026 Solemnity of the Holy Apostles Peter and Paul LEO PP. XIV A.2 Fr. DICASTERY FOR THE DOCTRINE OF THE FAITH Prot. N. 99/2009 DECREE Despite the warnings addressed to the Superior General of the Priestly Society of Saint Pius X, Bishop Alfonso de Galarreta, having committed an act of a schismatic nature through the episcopal consecration of four priests, without a pontifical mandate and against the will of the Supreme Pontiff, has ipso facto incurred the penalties provided for by canon 1387 and canon 1364 § 1 CIC 2021. I therefore declare for all legal purposes that both the aforementioned Bishop Alfonso de Galarreta and Pascal Schreiber, Michael Goldade, Michel Poinsinet de Sivry and Marc Hanappier have incurred ipso facto the latae sententiae excommunication reserved to the Apostolic See. I further declare that Bishop Bernard Fellay, having directly participated in the liturgical celebration as a co-consecrator, thus publicly adhering to the schismatic act, has incurred the latae sententiae excommunication provided for by canon 1364 § 1 CIC 2021. Clerics and lay faithful are warned not to adhere to the schism of the Priestly Society of Saint Pius X, because they would ipso facto incur the penalty of latae sententiae excommunication. From the Dicastery Building, 2 July 2026 Víctor M. Card. Fernández, Prefect John J. DICASTERY FOR THE DOCTRINE OF THE FAITH Prot. N. 99/2009 EXPLANATORY NOTE From the time of Saint Paul VI until the most recent discussions held at this Dicastery, multiple attempts to bring the members of the movement initiated by Archbishop Marcel Lefebvre back into full communion with the Catholic Church have proven futile. This situation has been further aggravated by recent episcopal consecrations celebrated without papal mandate, against the will of the Holy Father, in open violation of canon law. Therefore, this Dicastery, in the faithful exercise of the functions entrusted to it, deems it necessary to note that this act constituted the crime of schism, with canonical consequences for the sacred ministers and lay faithful involved. Indeed, as already declared in 1988, "such disobedience — which entails a practical rejection of the Roman Primacy — constitutes a schismatic act" (cf. John Paul II, Apostolic Letter Ecclesia Dei, 3). In this regard, from now on: 1. The sacred ministers belonging to the Priestly Fraternity of Saint Pius X are in schism and must therefore be considered schismatic (cf. Ecclesia Dei, 5c; Pontifical Council for the Interpretation of Legislative Texts, Explanatory Note on the excommunication for schism incurred by the adherents of the movement of Bishop Marcel Lefebvre, 24.08.1996, 5–6), resulting in them being subject to the excommunication provided for by law (can. 1364 §1 CIC). 2. As regards the lay faithful, those who formally adhere to the Priestly Fraternity of Saint Pius X under the conditions established in the Explanatory Note of the Pontifical Council for the Interpretation of Legislative Texts of 1996 (cf. ibid., 7), still in force, which this Dicastery adopts, are to be considered schismatic and excommunicated. 3. Finally, the holy People of God are warned that the sacred ministers of the Priestly Fraternity of Saint Pius X administer the sacraments illicitly and that the sacrament of penance administered by them and the marriage assisted by them are invalid. The Church, as a caring mother, will welcome with sincere affection and deep concern all those who wish to return to full communion. The Apostolic Nuncios will have access to the procedures that Ordinaries may use in various cases. Finally, all the faithful are exhorted to remain firm in communion with the Roman Pontiff, with the Bishops in communion with him and with the whole Church (cf. Lumen Gentium, 22; can. 751 CIC), and to abstain from participating in the celebrations and activities promoted by the aforementioned Priestly Fraternity of Saint Pius X.
